mods.abstract: In the late 18th century epigraphers began to take note of the inscriptions on Mount Gang. The first was Ruan Yuan 阮元 (1764–1849), who together with the older Bi Yuan 畢元 (1730-1797) compiled his pioneering Shanzuo jinshi zhi 山左金石志 (Epigraphical Records of Shandong) in 1796 when he was Director of Education in Shandong. As a high official he did not venture to the sites himself, but worked on the basis of rubbings that were brought or shown to him by colleagues. One of them was the scholar and antiquarian Huang Yi 黄易 (1744-1802), an early field archaeologist, who excavated and restored the Wu family shrines in Shandong. note: Ruan Yuan says, for example, that the rubbing of the stele on Mount Shuiniu was in Huang Yi’s collection, and he complains that Huang Yi did not provide him with exact measurement of the cliff inscriptions on Mount Shuiniu. Shanzuo jinshi zhi, ch. 10 (Shike shiliao xinbian), 1, 19: 14486. Huang Yi’s restoration of the Wu family shrines was a major accomplishment in the early history of archaeology in China. See the pathbreaking study by Wu Hung, The Wu Liang Shrine. The Ideology of Early Chinese Pictorial Art. (Stanford: Stanford University Press, 1989). and more recently Michael Nylan, Anthony Barbieri-Low, et al., Recarving China’s past: art, archaeology, and architecture of the "Wu Family Shrines." Ed. Cary A. Liu, (New Haven, London: Yale University Press, 2005). (LL)
